quote: Insight lives in cognition. Change lives in capacity.
through_line: Knowing how something works and being able to build it are different things — and building it requires more than understanding the pattern. This runs through agent skill design, maker education, and the psychology of change: comprehension is a starting condition, not a finishing line.

articles.title: Agent Skills
articles.pitch: Willison examines Anthropic's skills mechanism — the emerging standard for giving agents a defined, reusable portfolio of capabilities. Skills are effectively subagent specifications: structured definitions that tell an orchestrator what a given agent can do and when to delegate to it. The piece connects this to broader questions of how to organise multi-agent systems around bounded, composable competencies — directly relevant to folder and context organisation for per-client agent setups.

articles.title: Spring AI Agentic Patterns (Part 4): Subagent Orchestration
articles.pitch: Spring's engineering blog walks through a concrete implementation of subagent orchestration using a Task tool inspired by Claude Code's subagent design. Each subagent is defined as a Markdown file in an `agents/` folder, specifying name, description, allowed tools, preferred model, and system prompt. This is the level of structural specificity — actual folders, files, formats — that turns conceptual architecture into something buildable. The most implementation-grounded piece in this week's Craft reading.

articles.title: Mastering Generative Engine Optimization in 2026: Full Guide
articles.pitch: Search Engine Land's full GEO guide is the practitioner's entry point into what's replacing traditional SEO for destination websites. The shift to answer-engine optimisation is now mainstream, but this guide gets specific: schema markup, authoritative question-answering formats, and how AI systems decide what to cite. Essential structure for any DMO web team rethinking content architecture for AI discoverability.

articles.title: Constructionism: A Learning Theory and a Model for Maker Education
articles.pitch: This piece from Stanford's FabLearn Fellows traces Papert's constructionism — learning is most effective when the learner constructs something external, not just receives information — and extends it to maker education. The creator-vs-consumer distinction is made explicit: children making games and tools learn more than children using them. A strong intellectual foundation for positioning a coding academy around intentional making, and for articulating that positioning to parents who see technology as threat rather than tool.

articles.title: Why Psychological Insight Doesn't Change People
articles.pitch: What most therapy and self-examination produces is not change but narrative fluency — better explanations for why we are the way we are, not a reorganisation of how we actually respond. The essay's core distinction is quiet but devastating: knowing why you do something is not the same as being able to do otherwise. Insight operates in cognition; change lives in capacity. A precise dissection of why understanding can function as a stopping point rather than a beginning.

articles.title: The Surprising Benefits of Giving Up
articles.pitch: Drawing on a recent meta-analysis, this Nautilus essay finds that disengaging from goals in the face of real adversity is not failure but adaptive intelligence — and is often associated with better psychological outcomes than continued persistence. A quietly subversive piece for a culture that treats effort and continuation as unconditional virtues. Particularly resonant alongside this week's Deep reading on the limits of knowing as a path to growth.
